questions.question: 100Base-TX uses a 125 Mbaud symbol stream to carry 100 Mbps of data. What is the name of the encoding that causes this 25% overhead?
questions.explanation: 4B/5B maps each 4-bit nibble to a 5-bit code group, expanding the stream by 5/4 = 1.25, so 100 Mbps of data requires a 125 Mbaud symbol rate. Manchester encoding doubles the baud rate; 8B/10B is used in Gigabit Ethernet; MLT-3 is the subsequent line signaling step, not the source of the 25% overhead.

questions.question: In the Fast Ethernet family, which PHY uses four Category 3 copper pairs instead of two pairs?
questions.explanation: 100Base-T4 was designed to use four Category 3 pairs with 8B/6T ternary signaling so that existing lower-grade building cabling could support 100 Mbps. 100Base-TX uses two Cat5 pairs, 100Base-FX uses two multimode fiber strands, and 100Base-SX does not exist in the standard.

questions.question: An MLT-3 encoder is in the 0 voltage state. It receives the encoded bit sequence 1 0 1 1 0. What voltage level does it output for the fifth bit (the trailing 0)?
options: The level depends on the previous transition direction
questions.explanation: MLT-3 advances the cycle (-1 -> 0 -> +1 -> 0 -> -1 ...) only on a 1 bit; a 0 bit holds the current level. Starting at state-index 0 (level -1 by the cycle), bit1=1 -> index1 (level 0), bit2=0 -> index1 (level 0), bit3=1 -> index2 (level +1), bit4=1 -> index3 (level 0), bit5=0 -> index3 (level 0). The fifth bit outputs 0.

questions.question: Device A advertises 10T-HD, 10T-FD, and 100TX-HD. Device B advertises 10T-FD and 100TX-FD. Which mode does auto-negotiation select?
questions.explanation: Auto-negotiation walks the priority list from highest to lowest: 100TX-FD is not in A's set, so skip; 100TX-HD is not in B's set, so skip; 10T-FD is in both sets — this is the first common mode and is selected. 10T-HD is lower priority and would only be chosen if 10T-FD were absent from one side.

questions.question: A server NIC is forced to 100/full-duplex. The connected switch port is set to auto-negotiate. What duplex mode does the switch port typically end up in, and why?
options: 100 Mbps full duplex, because it reads the forced mode from the wire
100 Mbps half duplex, because the forced side suppresses Fast Link Pulses and the auto side cannot detect duplex without them
10 Mbps half duplex, because no FLPs means the auto side falls back to 10 Mbps
The link stays down, because FLPs are required for any link to form
questions.explanation: When one side is forced, it suppresses Fast Link Pulse (FLP) bursts. The auto side can infer the line rate from signal frequency but has no FLP data to determine duplex. Per the 802.3 spec the auto side defaults to half duplex, producing a classic duplex mismatch: the full-duplex NIC sees FCS errors and the half-duplex switch port sees late collisions.

questions.question: A 100Base-TX link is up at the correct speed, but one interface shows rising FCS errors and the other shows rising late collisions. What is the most likely root cause?
options: A broken fiber strand on the receive path
A duplex mismatch: one side is full duplex and the other is half duplex
Excessive cable length causing symbol errors across the whole link
An MTU mismatch between the two devices
questions.explanation: The asymmetric counter pattern — FCS/align errors on one side and late collisions on the other — is the textbook signature of a duplex mismatch. The full-duplex side transmits without listening for collisions, so the half-duplex side detects a collision after its slot time and increments late_collisions. The resulting truncated frames arrive at the full-duplex receiver as FCS errors. A fiber break would take the link down; cable length issues produce symmetric errors; MTU mismatches show up at IP/transport, not in PHY counters.
